[2024-12-02 21:42] .holyfire (Holy-Fire): Basically what I want to model is the following:

Both players start out building drones. The strategy each player chooses is the time t at which they stop building drones and build tarsiers instead. (There's no sense in building drones again after you started with tarsiers).

In all cases, a player which is attacked defends in the most efficient way - with absorb when it's available, or soak when max absorb is reached.

I want to find the time t which is a Nash equilibrium. This will be the optimal time no player will want to deviate from.

From this concept it's just working out the solutions to the equations you get at each phase, and thus the initial conditions of the last phase, from which you can calculate which player eventually cannot defend and effectively loses.

[2024-12-03 15:23] .holyfire (Holy-Fire): We already talked about that... You're right in principle, but not in practice. Even though a wall is effectively 2.22 BV, there is no point in the game where the cost per BV is actually 2.22. At first, because a wall is too big and a single wall gives you more soak than you need. Then, because you need to build ~1 engineer per turn for granularity. And then, even though you're facing more attack and 1 engineer is a small part of that - you're not going to actually defend with more walls, because you only have 1 blastforge, you're probably not going to build more at this point in the game, and your supply of walls is limited. So you need to defend less efficiently - engineers, FF, Rhino etc. - increasing the marginal cost per BV.

If you want to model all this accurately, then the concept of "BV" is completely meaningless. You just look at the actual game tree and whatever is the result of minimaxing it is the correct play.

If you want to model it abstractly and simply, it's very reasonable to encapsulate all these inefficiencies with a single BV value that is higher than the theoretical 2.22.

I guess another approach is to model BV non-linearly. Instead of Soak_Cost = 2.4 * Soak_Amount, you can have Soak_Cost = 2.4 * Soak_Amount + 0.02 * Soak_Amount^2. This models the increasingly inefficient defense as the game progresses.
[2024-12-03 15:24] .holyfire (Holy-Fire): Of course, in the continuous model, non-linear defense cost makes the relevant differential equations harder, but I think they're still solvable.

I've adopted the "X is a bigger number than Y" phrase and use it all the time.

[2024-12-27 04:30] silentslayers: Very much depends. When in doubt, try to calculate the next 1 or 2 turns cause usually the game states are smallish in these breach scenarios. Main rule of thumb is 2 drones = 1 attacker. AKA don't target splitters (usually), target drones/tarsiers. tarsiers ofc hurt you 1 turn earlier than their drones do stuff, so you can argue kill tarsier in most cases, but it does come down to how good that damage of theirs is, and what the drones are buying. And depends if you can continuously breach them or if it's just a 1 time breach and the game's tempo changes afterwards.

__Some examples:__

General case -> Kill tarsiers

When there's vivids -> Kill vivids only if they buy good stuff (example: walls, breach proof stuff) **and** killing their tarsiers doesn't save anything valuable of yours. Otherwise, kill tarsiers

If you're good at counting -> Calculate 1-2 turns ahead, try to leave them at exactly 4 gold so they can't wall (for example), leave them low enough in drones so they can't start absorbing again, put them at a bad attack number so it's wasted. Breakpoint stuff like that. 

If you can only breach once and the game will slow down afterwards (freeze or burst from something like wincer etc) -> Kill drones
